Explore the `apps/nas-mcp` directory of the repo at /worksp/monitor/app very thoroughly. I'm writing an implementation plan to add 5 new MCP operations: `start_file_inventory`, `schedule_file_inventory`, `get_file_inventory_status`, `fetch_file_inventory_result`, `cancel_file_inventory`. These call the nas-api HTTP service. Some are "tier 1" (auto-execute, read-only) and some are "tier 2" (require preview + approval). I need to know EXACTLY how operations/tools are defined and registered so a new engineer can add these following existing conventions.

Report back with specific file paths, line numbers, and short code excerpts:

1. **Language & layout**: Language (TS?), entrypoint (the CLAUDE.md mentions apps/nas-mcp/src/index.ts), full directory tree with one-line purpose per significant file.

2. **The MCP server & exposed tools**: CLAUDE.md says there are 119 tools in ALL_TOOL_DEFS but only 5 exposed per session (tool_search, invoke_tool, run_command, check_disk_space, restart_nas_api). Explain this architecture precisely. Where is ALL_TOOL_DEFS defined? What is the shape of a single tool definition object (every field — name, description, tier/safety label, parameters/schema, handler, etc.)? Quote a complete real example of one tool definition.

3. **tools-config.json**: Where is it, what's its schema, how does it control which tools are enabled? Show its relevant structure.

4. **Tiering / safety labels**: How exactly is the tier (1/2/3) or safety label expressed on a tool definition? How does tier-2 "preview + HMAC approval" actually work in code? Where is the preview generated and where is approval checked? Quote the relevant code.

5. **How invoke_tool / tool_search work**: How does invoke_tool dispatch to a specific tool from ALL_TOOL_DEFS? How does tool_search surface tools? Show the dispatch code.

6. **Calling nas-api**: Do existing tools call the nas-api HTTP service? If so show the HTTP client pattern (base URL from env, auth header, how a NAS is targeted — since there are 2 NAS units edgesynology1/edgesynology2). How does the MCP server know which NAS to talk to? Quote a real example tool that proxies to nas-api.

7. **Adding a new tool — exact recipe**: Based on everything, what are the precise steps and files to touch to add ONE new operation that proxies to a nas-api endpoint, with correct tier? List every file and the edit.

8. **restart_nas_api special case**: CLAUDE.md says restart_nas_api is implemented directly in index.ts (not via ALL_TOOL_DEFS). Show how, since one of our new tools might need similar treatment.

9. **Build & test**: Dockerfile, build command, how tests are written/run, example test.

10. **Parameter schema validation**: How are tool input parameters validated (zod? JSON schema?)? Show an example.

Be concrete and quote real code. Medium-thorough depth.

content.content:
1	# NAS MCP Server
2	
3	Exposes Synology NAS diagnostic + remediation tools to AI chat clients (claude.ai, Claude Desktop) via the Model Context Protocol over Streamable HTTP at `/mcp`. Legacy SSE remains served at `/sse`.
4	
5	## Connection
6	
7	| | |
8	|---|---|
9	| **URL** | `https://nas-mcp.designflow.app/mcp` |
10	| **Transport** | Streamable HTTP |
11	| **Legacy URL** | `https://nas-mcp.designflow.app/sse` |
12	| **Legacy transport** | SSE (still served, but prefer `/mcp`) |
13	| **Auth** | `Authorization: Bearer <MCP_BEARER_TOKEN>` |
14	
15	Claude Desktop / claude.ai client config:
16	
17	```json
18	{
19	  "nas-mcp": {
20	    "url": "https://nas-mcp.designflow.app/mcp",
21	    "type": "streamable-http",
22	    "headers": { "Authorization": "Bearer <MCP_BEARER_TOKEN>" }
23	  }
24	}
25	```
26	
27	**After any redeploy** (Coolify restarts the container), all in-memory MCP sessions are gone. Existing conversations holding a `mcp-session-id` will see a 404. Start a new conversation to get a fresh session.
28	
29	## Tool surface — lazy-loaded registry
30	
31	The server has a registry of 119 shared tool definitions but exposes only **5 tools** to MCP clients per session. This keeps the always-loaded `tools/list` surface at ~3k tokens (vs ~50k if all 119 were registered upfront). `restart_nas_api` is one of the 5 always-on tools and is implemented directly in `src/index.ts`, so enabled-tool counts in `tools-config.json` can be one higher than `ALL_TOOL_DEFS`.
32	
33	| Always-on tool | Purpose |
34	|---|---|
35	| `tool_search({ query, limit })` | Search the registry by keyword. Returns names, descriptions, and parameter shapes as text. **Call this first** to discover the right tool. |
36	| `invoke_tool({ name, target, args })` | Execute any registry tool by name. For write tools, include `confirmed: true` inside `args`. |
37	| `run_command({ target, command })` | Free-form tier-1-only shell. Write commands are blocked by the NAS API validator. |
38	| `check_disk_space({ target })` | Eager freebie — disk + inode usage across volumes. |
39	| `restart_nas_api({ target, confirmed })` | Eager freebie — recovery tool, always available. |
40	
41	### Typical flow
42	
43	```text
44	tool_search({ query: "snapshot recovery" })
45	  → returns: list_snapshot_candidates, create_prechange_snapshot,
46	             restore_path_from_snapshot, inspect_recycle_bin, ...
47	
48	invoke_tool({
49	  name: "list_snapshot_candidates",
50	  target: "edgesynology1",
51	  args: { filter: "drive" }
52	})
53	```
54	
55	### Search keywords + groups
56	
57	`tool_search` matches against:
58	- **Keyword index** (`KEYWORD_TO_GROUPS` in `src/tool-definitions.ts`): `snapshot`, `backup`, `drive`, `sync`, `sharesync`, `disk`, `smart`, `btrfs`, `scrub`, `network`, `tailscale`, `bond`, `dns`, `memory`, `cpu`, `performance`, `iowait`, `log`, `package`, `restart`, `file`, `permission`, `acl`, `delete`, `recover`, `recycle`, `security`, `session`, `temperature`, `volume`, `space`, `audit`, `task`, `scheduled`
59	- **Group names**: `system`, `performance`, `network`, `security`, `drive_sync`, `logs`, `storage`, `files`, `recovery`, `packages`, `backup`, `write_restart`, `write_storage`, `write_files`, `write_tasks`, `misc`
60	- **Name substring** of any tool in the registry
61	- **Description substring** of any tool
62	
63	Multi-word queries require a tool to match **all** words (AND, not OR). When a query word is an exact group name (e.g. `files`, `recovery`), only tools in that group are returned — description matches for that word are suppressed. For all other words, tools in KEYWORD_TO_GROUPS-mapped groups plus name/description substring matches qualify. Survivors are scored: name match +3, description match +1 per word; sorted descending then alphabetically. Default `limit: 8`, max 30.
64	
65	### Why lazy-load, why not dynamic registration
66	
67	MCP supports `notifications/tools/list_changed`, but Claude clients cache the initial `tools/list` and do not re-fetch on the notification. So `tool_search` cannot register new tools mid-session. Returning schemas as text (which Claude then uses to construct an `invoke_tool` call) is the only mechanism that actually delivers the context savings on real clients.
68	
69	The server is also fully stateless (no `mcp-session-id`), so any dynamic registration would be discarded at the end of the request anyway.
70	
71	## NAS targets
72	
73	Every registry tool accepts a `target`:
74	
75	| Value | Meaning |
76	|---|---|
77	| `edgesynology1` | Run on NAS 1 only |
78	| `edgesynology2` | Run on NAS 2 only |
79	| `both` | Run on both NAS boxes in parallel |
80	
81	When `both` is used, results are labeled `[edgesynology1]` / `[edgesynology2]`. On a loaded NAS, prefer targeting a single NAS to avoid the 45s tool deadline being reached while waiting for the slow side.
82	
83	## Tool tiers and approval
84	
85	Enforced by the NAS API:
86	
87	| Tier | Type | Approval |
88	|---|---|---|
89	| 1 | Read-only diagnostic | Automatic |
90	| 2 | State-modifying (reversible) | `confirmed: true` inside `args` |
91	| 3 | Destructive / irreversible | `confirmed: true` + HMAC-signed token (issued by the MCP server) |
92	
93	Calling `invoke_tool` on a write tool without `confirmed: true` returns a preview of the exact shell command that would run, prefixed with the NAS name. Re-call with `confirmed: true` to execute.
94	
95	## Timeout architecture
96	
97	Three layers protect against NAS-side hangs:
98	
99	| Layer | Timeout | What it does |
100	|---|---|---|
101	| `/preview` HTTP abort | 8s | Aborts the tier-classification call |
102	| `/exec` HTTP abort | 25s command + 5s buffer = 30s | Aborts the execution call; sends `timeout_ms: 25000` to nas-api so it kills the subprocess first |
103	| Tool deadline | 45s | If both NASes stall, the MCP tool returns a clear error at 45s rather than holding the connection until Claude's 4-minute client timeout |
104	
105	HTTP client uses `AbortController` + `setTimeout` (not `AbortSignal.timeout()`) and sets `Connection: close` to prevent undici keep-alive pool exhaustion.
106	
107	The Node HTTP server has `keepAliveTimeout: 120s` / `headersTimeout: 125s` — above Traefik's 90s idle — so Traefik never reuses a connection Node has already closed.
108	
109	## Enabling / disabling tools
110	
111	`tools-config.json` controls which registry tools are invokable:
112	
113	```json
114	{
115	  "enabled_read_tools":  [ "check_disk_space", "check_btrfs_detail", ... ],
116	  "enabled_write_tools": [ "restart_monitor_agent", "start_btrfs_scrub", ... ]
117	}
118	```
119	
120	Tools in the registry but not listed are compiled into the image but invisible to `tool_search` and rejected by `invoke_tool` with a "disabled in tools-config.json" message. This lets you ship a tool dark and enable it without a code deploy — edit the JSON and push. The special always-on `restart_nas_api` entry is enabled in this file but does not live in `ALL_TOOL_DEFS`.
121	
122	Changes take effect on the next image build (push to `main` → GitHub Actions → Coolify redeploy).
123	
124	## Registry catalog
125	
126	The lists below describe what's in `ALL_TOOL_DEFS` for discovery purposes. All are invoked via `invoke_tool({ name, target, args })`, except `check_disk_space` / `restart_nas_api` / `run_command` which are also directly callable as always-on tools.
127	
128	### Group `system` (11)
129	
130	| Tool | What it does |
131	|---|---|
132	| `check_system_info` | DSM version, NAS model, uptime, CPU, memory — baseline context |
133	| `check_disk_space` | Disk + inode usage for all active volumes (also always-on) |
134	| `check_hardware_temps` | CPU/chassis temperatures, fan speeds, SMART disk temperatures |
135	| `check_volume_health` | DSM RAID/volume health, mdstat, SMART |
136	| `check_packages` | All installed DSM packages, running status, recent events |
137	| `check_scheduled_tasks` | All DSM scheduled tasks and last run result |
138	| `list_volumes` | Active data volumes + filesystem usage |
139	| `list_shared_folders` | Share names → real filesystem paths |
140	| `inspect_mounts` | Live mount graph for data, package, and bind mounts |
141	| `inspect_encryption_state` | Whether volumes/share paths are mounted + visible |
142	| `check_agent_container` | Whether the monitor agent container is running |
143	
144	### Group `performance` (5)
145	
146	| Tool | What it does |
147	|---|---|
148	| `check_cpu_iowait` | CPU I/O wait % |
149	| `get_resource_snapshot` | Full live picture: top processes, disk I/O, connections, memory, recent errors |
150	| `check_io_stalls` | Processes stuck on disk, queue depth, hung-task warnings |
151	| `check_memory_detail` | Full meminfo, swap activity, dirty/writeback, OOM kills |
152	| `check_container_io` | Docker containers doing the most disk I/O |
153	
154	### Group `network` (8)
155	
156	| Tool | What it does |
157	|---|---|
158	| `check_network_health` | Interface errors/drops, routing, DNS, listening ports |
159	| `check_tailscale` | Tailscale interface state, IP, daemon reachability |
160	| `check_network_connections` | Active TCP per process, state counts, top peers |

166	
167	### Group `security` (2)
168	
169	| Tool | What it does |
170	|---|---|
171	| `check_security_log` | Failed logins, security events, admin audit, SSH |

174	### Group `drive_sync` (9)
175	
176	| Tool | What it does |

201

269	### Group `write_tasks` (5)
270	
271	`generate_support_bundle`, `trigger_backup_task`, `run_scheduled_task`, `enable_scheduled_task`, `disable_scheduled_task`
272	
273	### Group `misc` (2 — untagged, still searchable)
274	
275	`kill_process`, `run_privileged_command`. These have intentionally narrow scope and fall through to "misc" via the substring + name fallback in `searchTools`.
276	
277	## Deployment
278	
279	Standard CI/CD path (see [AI_OPERATING_RULES.md](../../AI_OPERATING_RULES.md)):
280	
281	- Push to `main` with changes under `apps/nas-mcp/**`
282	- `.github/workflows/nas-mcp-image.yml` builds and pushes `ghcr.io/u2giants/synology-monitor-nas-mcp:latest`
283	- Coolify auto-deploys (app UUID `efl17f5iocnz94840pexre9d`, project Synology Monitor → production)
284	
285	## Environment variables (set in Coolify)
286	
287	| Variable | Purpose |
288	|---|---|
289	| `MCP_PORT` | Port the server listens on (default `3001`) |
290	| `MCP_BEARER_TOKEN` | Auth token required by all MCP clients |
291	| `NAS_EDGE1_NAME` | Logical name for NAS 1 (default `edgesynology1`) |
292	| `NAS_EDGE1_API_URL` | HTTP URL of NAS 1 API (`http://100.107.131.35:7734`) |
293	| `NAS_EDGE1_API_SECRET` | Bearer secret for NAS 1 API |
294	| `NAS_EDGE1_API_SIGNING_KEY` | HMAC key for tier 2/3 approval tokens on NAS 1 |
295	| `NAS_EDGE2_NAME` | Logical name for NAS 2 (default `edgesynology2`) |
296	| `NAS_EDGE2_API_URL` | HTTP URL of NAS 2 API (`http://100.107.131.36:7734`) |
297	| `NAS_EDGE2_API_SECRET` | Bearer secret for NAS 2 API |
298	| `NAS_EDGE2_API_SIGNING_KEY` | HMAC key for tier 2/3 approval tokens on NAS 2 |
299	
300	## Adding a tool
301	
302	1. Add a new `McpToolDef` entry to `ALL_TOOL_DEFS` in `src/tool-definitions.ts`.
303	2. Tag it: add `<tool_name>: "<group>"` to `TOOL_GROUPS` in the same file. (Skipping this is allowed — it falls into `"misc"` and startup logs a warning.)
304	3. Enable it: add the name to `enabled_read_tools` or `enabled_write_tools` in `tools-config.json`.
305	4. `pnpm build` to type-check locally.
306	5. Push to `main`. No changes to `index.ts` needed unless the tool should be eagerly registered (in which case add it to `EAGER_TOOLS`).

66	 * Handles login, the API call, result check, and logout.

69	function buildDsmApiCall(
70	  api: string,
71	  version: number,
72	  method: string,
73	  extraArgs: string[], // each entry is a --data-urlencode "key=value" fragment (value may reference $SHELL_VAR)
74	  description: string,
75	): string {
76	  return [
77	    `if [ -z "\${DSM_USERNAME:-}" ] || [ -z "\${DSM_PASSWORD:-}" ]; then echo "ERROR: DSM_USERNAME/DSM_PASSWORD not set in .env — required for WebAPI calls"; exit 1; fi`,
78	    `DSM_BASE="http://localhost:\${DSM_PORT:-5000}/webapi/entry.cgi"`,
79	    `echo "=== Authenticating to DSM WebAPI ==="`,
80	    `SID=$(curl -sfG "$DSM_BASE" --data-urlencode "api=SYNO.API.Auth" --data-urlencode "version=7" --data-urlencode "method=login" --data-urlencode "account=\${DSM_USERNAME}" --data-urlencode "passwd=\${DSM_PASSWORD}" --data-urlencode "format=sid" 2>/dev/null | grep -o '"sid":"[^"]*"' | cut -d'"' -f4)`,
81	    `if [ -z "$SID" ]; then echo "ERROR: DSM login failed — check DSM_USERNAME/DSM_PASSWORD and port \${DSM_PORT:-5000}"; exit 1; fi`,
82	    `echo "Authenticated"`,
83	    `echo ""`,
84	    `echo "=== ${description} (${api} v${version} method=${method}) ==="`,
85	    `RESULT=$(curl -sfG "$DSM_BASE" --data-urlencode "api=${api}" --data-urlencode "version=${version}" --data-urlencode "method=${method}" ${extraArgs.join(" ")} --data-urlencode "_sid=$SID" 2>/dev/null)`,
86	    `echo "$RESULT"`,
87	    `echo "$RESULT" | grep -q '"success":true' && echo "OK" || echo "Non-success — check DSM for errors"`,
88	    `echo ""`,
89	    `curl -sfG "$DSM_BASE" --data-urlencode "api=SYNO.API.Auth" --data-urlencode "version=7" --data-urlencode "method=logout" --data-urlencode "_sid=$SID" >/dev/null 2>&1 || true`,
90	    `echo "Session closed"`,
91	  ].join("\n");
92	}

97	 * Validator classifies this as Tier 2 because it matches the SYNO.Core.Package
98	 * write-method pattern.

100	function buildDsmPackageRestart(packageId: string): string {
101	  return [
102	    `if [ -z "\${DSM_USERNAME:-}" ] || [ -z "\${DSM_PASSWORD:-}" ]; then echo "ERROR: DSM_USERNAME/DSM_PASSWORD not set in .env — required for WebAPI package restarts"; exit 1; fi`,
103	    `DSM_BASE="http://localhost:\${DSM_PORT:-5000}/webapi/entry.cgi"`,
104	    `echo "=== Authenticating to DSM WebAPI ==="`,
105	    `SID=$(curl -sfG "$DSM_BASE" --data-urlencode "api=SYNO.API.Auth" --data-urlencode "version=7" --data-urlencode "method=login" --data-urlencode "account=\${DSM_USERNAME}" --data-urlencode "passwd=\${DSM_PASSWORD}" --data-urlencode "format=sid" 2>/dev/null | grep -o '"sid":"[^"]*"' | cut -d'"' -f4)`,
106	    `if [ -z "$SID" ]; then echo "ERROR: DSM login failed — check DSM_USERNAME/DSM_PASSWORD and that DSM is reachable on port \${DSM_PORT:-5000}"; exit 1; fi`,
107	    `echo "Authenticated to DSM"`,
108	    `echo ""`,
109	    `echo "=== Stopping ${packageId} via SYNO.Core.Package method=stop ==="`,
110	    `STOP=$(curl -sfG "$DSM_BASE" --data-urlencode "api=SYNO.Core.Package" --data-urlencode "version=1" --data-urlencode "method=stop" --data-urlencode "id=${packageId}" --data-urlencode "_sid=$SID" 2>/dev/null)`,
111	    `echo "$STOP"`,
112	    `echo "$STOP" | grep -q '"success":true' && echo "Stop: OK" || echo "Stop: non-success (package may already be stopped)"`,
113	    `echo ""`,
114	    `sleep 4`,
115	    `echo "=== Starting ${packageId} via SYNO.Core.Package method=start ==="`,
116	    `START=$(curl -sfG "$DSM_BASE" --data-urlencode "api=SYNO.Core.Package" --data-urlencode "version=1" --data-urlencode "method=start" --data-urlencode "id=${packageId}" --data-urlencode "_sid=$SID" 2>/dev/null)`,
117	    `echo "$START"`,
118	    `echo "$START" | grep -q '"success":true' && echo "Start: OK" || echo "Start: non-success — check DSM Package Center for errors"`,
119	    `echo ""`,
120	    `curl -sfG "$DSM_BASE" --data-urlencode "api=SYNO.API.Auth" --data-urlencode "version=7" --data-urlencode "method=logout" --data-urlencode "_sid=$SID" >/dev/null 2>&1 || true`,
121	    `echo "Session closed"`,
122	  ].join("\n");

content.text: Now let me check the Dockerfile and package.json to understand build/test process:

content.content:
1	# NAS MCP server — exposes NAS diagnostic tools to AI agents via MCP over SSE.
2	# Build context is the REPO ROOT (not apps/nas-mcp): the tool catalog is the
3	# single source in packages/shared, reached via the src/nas-tools.ts symlink, so
4	# the build must see packages/shared. tsc resolves the symlink in place
5	# (preserveSymlinks) and emits dist/nas-tools.js, keeping dist/index.js intact.
6	FROM node:22-alpine AS builder
7	WORKDIR /app/apps/nas-mcp
8	COPY apps/nas-mcp/package.json .
9	RUN npm install
10	COPY apps/nas-mcp/tsconfig.json .
11	COPY apps/nas-mcp/src/ ./src/
12	# Shared tool catalog (single source) — the symlink apps/nas-mcp/src/nas-tools.ts
13	# points at ../../../packages/shared/src/nas-tools.ts, so it must exist here.
14	COPY packages/shared/src/ /app/packages/shared/src/
15	RUN npm run build
16	
17	FROM node:22-alpine AS runner
18	WORKDIR /app/apps/nas-mcp
19	RUN apk add --no-cache curl
20	COPY --from=builder /app/apps/nas-mcp/dist ./dist
21	COPY --from=builder /app/apps/nas-mcp/node_modules ./node_modules
22	# tools-config.json is copied at build time.
23	# To change which tools are enabled: edit the file, push to GitHub,
24	# and Coolify will automatically rebuild and redeploy this container.
25	COPY apps/nas-mcp/tools-config.json .
26	EXPOSE 3001
27	CMD ["node", "dist/index.js"]
28
